NameTranslationKey.Description: Translation key for NPC name display

Instructions.$Comment: Wave at player when they approach
Watching state - player is nearby, just watch them

Watch nearby players with head - but NOT while fighting/chasing a hostile (see the And+Not below). 2026-07-22: this node has Continue:true so it ran every tick regardless of what else matched, competing with the melee/chase combat nodes' own HeadMotion:Watch (which watches the locked hostile Target, confirmed via disassembly of HeadMotionWatch.computeSteering() - it resolves its target from THIS node's own Sensor-derived InfoProvider, not some single global 'last write wins' state) - live-reported bug: the companion kept looking at its owner instead of the monster it was fighting/charging at. Fixed by making this node simply not match at all while any hostile is locked (Target sensor with a generously large Range, functioning as 'is anything currently locked as Target regardless of distance') rather than relying on unverified assumptions about which of two simultaneously-matching HeadMotion assignments would win.

"AI process message waiting" indicator: a floating thought-bubble particle above this NPC's head while npc-ai-stack is waiting on a real orchestrator round trip - a reply that just appears in chat with zero visual buildup reads as unnaturally instant. Real, shipped particle asset (Server/Particles/NPC/Emotions/Question_Subtle.particlesystem); Offset [0, 2.1, 0] confirmed correct for this Kweebec-based model via the real, shipped Component_Kweebec_Instruction_Search.json (same offset, its own 'Question' particle). Re-fire throttling lives in IsAwaitingReplySensor.java itself (a 2s per-NPC cooldown), NOT the JSON "Once" modifier - confirmed via disassembly and live testing that "Once" only means "once per STATE ENTRY" here (paired with an unconditional "Any" sensor in every real shipped usage), not "once per rising edge of a condition" - this condition can flip true/false many times within one uninterrupted Watching visit (once per chat message), so "Once" latched after the first reply and never fired again; see IsAwaitingReplySensor.java's docstring for the full story.

Adventurer-specific: notice nearby hostile creatures, feeds npc-ai-stack's ThreatMemory so the AI can react to it in conversation. LockOnTarget makes the found hostile the locked "Target" the chase/attack nodes below react to. 2026-07-21: switched the real gating to a custom "IsHostileSpecies" Filter (EntityFilterHostileSpecies.java) instead of relying on the Attitude Prioritiser's pairwise AttitudesByPriority match, after "npc must be hostile to all enemy" turned up a second, deeper bug than the earlier [Hostile,Neutral] widening fixed: WorldSupport.getAttitude() (AttitudeView.getAttitude, confirmed via disassembly) resolves PAIRWISE - per-entity override, then an AttitudeGroup lookup (each species' own shipped "Groups" JSON lists which OTHER group names it holds Hostile toward), then finally falls back to the OBSERVER's (Adventurer's) own DefaultNPCAttitude ("Ignore"). Fetched the real shipped AttitudeGroup assets live: only Trork.json lists "Kweebec" (Adventurer's AttitudeGroup) as Hostile - Goblin.json's Hostile list is empty, Outlander.json has no Hostile key at all, Scarak.json is hostile only to "Feran". So the pairwise system only ever recognized Trork as an enemy; every other hostile species fell through to Adventurer's own "Ignore" default and was silently excluded (an unlisted attitude reaching SensorEntityPrioritiserAttitude.getPriority() throws IllegalStateException - confirmed it's excluded upstream via the Prioritiser's own buildProvidedFilters(), an implicit inclusion filter). IsHostileSpecies instead reads the CANDIDATE's own DefaultPlayerAttitude directly (the one flag every dangerous vanilla mob sets consistently, since it's literally what makes them attack players) - a species-agnostic signal, not a narrow rivalry lookup. AttitudesByPriority widened to include "Ignore" (plus Friendly/Revered for safety) so the Prioritiser's own implicit filter never rejects a candidate IsHostileSpecies already matched - it now only ranks among survivors (pairwise-Hostile ranked above pairwise-Ignore, a reasonable tiebreak when multiple threats are present). Not yet live-confirmed against a non-Trork hostile (Goblin/Outlander/Scarak) - see hytale-plugin/README.md.

Reactive companion defense: real, shipped 'EntityEvent' sensor (EventType: Damage, SearchType: PlayerOnly, NPCGroup: Player - confirmed valid asset at Server/NPC/Groups/Player.json) - confirmed via disassembly of SensorEntityEvent/BuilderSensorEntityEvent AND via its real usage in Server/NPC/Roles/_Core/Components/Steps/Component_Instruction_Allied_Damage_Check.json. Fires the instant a nearby player takes damage, regardless of whether THIS companion currently has line of sight on whatever's hitting them - the Mob+IsHostileSpecies sensor above only reacts to a hostile the companion can already see/sense itself, which could miss an attacker sneaking up on a player specifically. Combined here with the same Mob+IsHostileSpecies hostile search, just widened to 45 blocks (vs. the normal 30) - we now know for certain a threat is imminent nearby, so it's worth searching harder for it. Uses the same IsHostileSpecies Filter + widened AttitudesByPriority as the block above - see its $Comment for why. NOT owner-scoped like the two follow blocks below - confirmed via disassembly that BuilderSensorEntityEvent extends BuilderSensorEvent, not BuilderSensorWithEntityFilters, so it has no candidate-iteration/Filters mechanism at all (a slot/subscription check, not an entity search) - there's no candidate Ref here to filter by owner. Documented limitation, not a bug: worst case is widening the hostile-search when a non-owner player is hit nearby; the actual lock/chase/attack target is still gated by IsHostileSpecies regardless, so this can't cause a wrong-target attack. Not yet live-confirmed (this whole companion-combat feature has never been tested near a real hostile creature - see hytale-plugin/README.md's "Known simplification").

Real, shipped 'Damage' sensor Type (Combat: true) - confirmed via disassembly of SensorDamage/BuilderSensorDamage AND via its real usage in Server/NPC/Roles/_Core/Components/Steps/Component_Instruction_Damage_Check.json (the neutral Kweebec's own Panic trigger). Reports a player physically attacking this NPC as a real outcome (personality.py's player_attacked_npc) - see NoteAttackedByPlayerAction.java. Not yet live-confirmed against a real attack (this NPC role sets Invulnerable:true; grounded in PlayerDamageFilterSystem's own pattern of cancelling damage via a flag on an event that still fires, rather than suppressing the event entirely, but that's inference from disassembly, not a live test).

Return to Idle when player leaves (clear animation first) - but NEVER for a tamed companion, regardless of how far its owner currently is. 2026-07-22 real bug found live ("tamed npc companion must always know where I am and always follow me"): this node has no Continue and sits ABOVE the follow/teleport nodes below, so if the owner exceeded 12 blocks and no OTHER player happened to be nearby, this fired FIRST and kicked the companion back to Idle state entirely - abandoning the follow/teleport logic before it could ever run, regardless of how generous their own Range values were. Idle state has no owner-tracking logic at all (just wave-if-a-player-approaches), so a companion that fell back to Idle this way would simply stand still forever once its owner got far enough away. Fixed by never leaving Watching for a companion at all - see the follow/teleport nodes below for how it actually stays with a far-away owner instead.

Companion combat, melee range: once tamed, if a hostile is locked as Target and close enough, attack it. Real "Attack" action (confirmed via disassembly of BuilderActionAttack, and via real shipped usage in Component_Instruction_Attack_Sequence_Spirit.json/Component_Instruction_Intelligent_Idle_Goblin.json) - "Root_NPC_Attack_Melee" is the same generic melee attack pattern the real, shipped Tamed_Cow.json uses, not a made-up interaction id. This node has no Continue, so it takes priority over chasing/following below whenever it matches.

Companion combat, chase: hostile locked as Target but not yet in melee range - close the distance. Takes priority over following the player (no Continue) whenever a hostile is being chased. HeadMotion:Watch added 2026-07-22 alongside the fix above that stops the player-watch node from competing during combat - watches the locked hostile (same Target sensor context the melee block below already uses for its own HeadMotion:Watch) so the companion visibly looks at what it's charging toward instead of having no head motion at all.

Guiding: the AI decided OFFER_GUIDE (see llm_client.py) and GuideState.startGuiding() was called - walk toward NearbyLandmarks.closestPosition() until SeekLandmarkSensor reports arrival (it stops matching automatically, no separate arrival check needed here). Takes priority over merely following the player (no Continue), but combat above still wins if a hostile is being fought/chased.

Companion mode, catch-up teleport: owner detected within a wide range but too far to walk to comfortably (beyond the normal 100-block follow range below). Real, shipped 'Teleport' BodyMotion (confirmed via the real, shipped Test_Teleport.json - the only place this engine's Teleport type is actually demonstrated; Template_Livestock.json's own vanilla tamed-animal state has no owner-tracking logic at all to model this on, since vanilla taming never needs to track a SPECIFIC player - this whole owner-teleport concept is custom to this project). 2026-07-22, added for "tamed npc companion must always know where I am and always follow me": self-limiting by construction, no cooldown/Java code needed - the moment it teleports the companion within 100 blocks, the very next tick's "Not within 100" check becomes false and this node stops matching, falling through to the normal walk-to-owner node below instead. OffsetRange/OffsetSector place it a few blocks from the owner in a random direction, not literally on top of them.

Companion mode: once tamed, seek toward ITS OWNER specifically (no hostile currently being fought/chased, and not actively guiding - see the nodes above, which take priority). The Seek/MaintainDistance BodyMotion types are real, shipped engine functionality (confirmed via disassembly and via the real Template_Livestock.json's own tamed-animal follow behavior), not custom pathfinding code. 2026-07-22: added the "IsOwner" Filter (EntityFilterIsOwner.java) to the Player sensor, replacing the earlier v1 simplification that followed the NEAREST player regardless of who actually tamed it - correct only when the owner happened to be nearest (true in all solo testing so far), wrong the moment another player is closer. Confirmed via disassembly that BuilderSensorPlayer extends BuilderSensorEntityBase, the same Filters-supporting base "Mob" uses (see EntityFilterHostileSpecies.java for the same pattern). CompanionState now tracks the real owner UUID, not just a companion boolean - see its javadoc. 2026-07-22, later: Range widened 30->100 and RelativeSpeed bumped 0.9->1.0 for "must always follow me" - at 0.9x speed the companion structurally fell behind during ANY sustained walking (let alone sprinting), eventually exceeding the old 30-block range and stopping outright; 100 blocks plus the teleport catch-up node above together mean normal separation (corners, sprinting ahead, minor terrain obstacles) never actually loses the companion.
Interaction state - look at the talking player

Same "AI process message waiting" indicator as the Watching state above - the very start of a click-triggered conversation's wait happens here, in $Interaction, before the ~1s Timeout below reverts to Watching.

Keep following even while starting a conversation - $Interaction only lasts ~1s before the Timeout below returns to Watching, but without this an established companion visibly freezes for that second every time the player clicks to talk. Continue:true is required here so the Timeout node below still runs every tick regardless of whether this one matches - without it, a nearby companion would get stuck in $Interaction forever. Same owner-scoped IsOwner Filter as the Watching state's matching block - see its $Comment for why. Range/RelativeSpeed matched to the Watching state's own 2026-07-22 widening (30->100, 0.9->1.0) for consistency - no teleport-catch-up node here though, since interacting requires the player to already be close enough to click in the first place, so the huge-separation case that node exists for can't apply during this specific ~1s window.
Return to Watching after interaction ends
